title: Pathology, Immunology & Infectious Disease
subtitle: How the body is injured, defends itself, and fails: cell injury, immunity, neoplasia, and infection
methodology: METHODOLOGY · Every entry anchors to a landmark study, paper, clinical case or theoretical text. The hint explains the idea in plain language; the note names the primary publication and flags replication status, effect-size revisions and known controversies. Studies and effects carry a replicability badge — 🟢 robust · 🟡 nuanced or reduced · 🔴 failed or severely contested. Map pins mark the institution where the work was carried out.

categories.label: Theories & Models
categories.subtitle: The frameworks that organise how disease arises, spreads, and is fought off by the body

entries.label: Koch's Postulates
entries.hint: A set of four criteria for proving a microorganism causes a specific disease: it must be found in every case, isolated in pure culture, reproduce the disease when introduced into a healthy host, and be re-isolated from that host. Robert Koch formalized these after using them to prove Bacillus anthracis caused anthrax and Mycobacterium tuberculosis caused tuberculosis. They turned "germ theory" from a plausible idea into a testable, falsifiable standard.
entries.note: Koch, R. (1884). 'Die Aetiologie der Tuberkulose.' Mittheilungen aus dem Kaiserlichen Gesundheitsamte, 2, 1–88. The postulates have been formally revised since — many real pathogens (e.g. viruses that need host cells to culture, asymptomatic carriers, polymicrobial disease) fail one or more criteria, and molecular "Koch's postulates" (Fredricks & Relman, 1996) were proposed to accommodate genomic-era pathogen detection. Still taught as the conceptual baseline for causal proof in infectious disease.

entries.label: Cellular Pathology
entries.hint: Rudolf Virchow's claim that all disease originates in disturbances at the level of the individual cell — "omnis cellula e cellula," every cell arises from a pre-existing cell — displacing the older humoral theory that disease came from imbalances in bodily fluids. It relocated the search for the cause of disease from the whole body down to the microscope, making cell injury the organizing unit of pathology.
entries.note: Virchow, R. (1858). Die Cellularpathologie in ihrer Begründung auf physiologische und pathologische Gewebelehre. Berlin: August Hirschwald. The theory itself is not something modern pathology has "failed to replicate" — it is the field's founding axiom, still structurally correct — but Virchow's specific mechanistic claims (e.g. about the origin of certain tumors) have been superseded by molecular genetics.

entries.label: Clonal Selection Theory
entries.hint: Each lymphocyte is pre-committed, before ever meeting an antigen, to recognise just one specific antigen shape; encountering that antigen triggers the cell to proliferate into a clone of identical antibody-producing offspring. Macfarlane Burnet's theory replaced the older "instructionist" idea that antigens physically mould antibodies, explaining both immune specificity and immunological memory as products of selection, not construction.
entries.note: Burnet, F.M. (1957). 'A modification of Jerne's theory of antibody production using the concept of clonal selection.' Australian Journal of Science, 20, 67–69. Confirmed decisively by later cellular and molecular immunology (single-cell antibody specificity, receptor gene rearrangement); Niels Jerne's earlier natural-selection framework (1955) anticipated part of the idea, and Burnet credited him in the title.

entries.label: Actively Acquired Immunological Tolerance
entries.hint: The immune system's ability to distinguish self from non-self is not fixed at birth — it can be actively taught. Exposing a mouse fetus to foreign cells before its immune system matures makes the adult mouse permanently tolerant of tissue from that same foreign donor. This showed self/non-self discrimination is a learned developmental process, not a hard-wired one, which underpins the entire logic of organ transplantation.
entries.note: Billingham, R.E., Brent, L., Medawar, P.B. (1953). 'Actively acquired tolerance of foreign cells.' Nature, 172, 603–606. Built on Ray Owen's 1945 observation that non-identical twin cattle sharing a placenta become natural blood-cell chimeras. Medawar shared the 1960 Nobel Prize with Burnet for this work; the tolerance mechanisms have since been substantially refined (central thymic deletion, peripheral regulatory T cells) beyond the original model.

entries.label: The Hallmarks of Cancer
entries.hint: A cancer cell must acquire a specific set of capabilities to become malignant: it makes its own growth signals, ignores signals to stop dividing, resists programmed cell death, achieves limitless replication, builds its own blood supply, and invades other tissue. Douglas Hanahan and Robert Weinberg's framework gave cancer biology a shared vocabulary for an otherwise bewilderingly diverse disease, organizing decades of scattered findings into one causal logic.
entries.note: Hanahan, D., Weinberg, R.A. (2000). 'The Hallmarks of Cancer.' Cell, 100(1), 57–70. The 2011 update ('Hallmarks of Cancer: The Next Generation,' Cell, 144(5), 646–674) added two more hallmarks directly relevant to this folio: reprogrammed energy metabolism (the Warburg effect) and evading immune destruction. Extremely widely cited and taught, though critics note it describes convergent end-states rather than a single unified mechanism.

entries.label: The Two-Hit Hypothesis
entries.hint: Some cancers require two separate mutational "hits" to the same gene — one on each copy inherited from each parent — before a cell loses control of its growth. Alfred Knudson inferred this from the statistics of retinoblastoma, a childhood eye tumour: inherited cases appear earlier and more often bilaterally than sporadic cases, exactly as predicted if inherited patients are already born with one hit and need only one more. This defined the concept of a tumour suppressor gene years before one was cloned.
entries.note: Knudson, A.G. (1971). 'Mutation and cancer: statistical study of retinoblastoma.' Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 68(4), 820–823. Confirmed at the molecular level in 1986 when the RB1 gene was cloned and shown to fit the two-hit pattern exactly. The model generalises well to other classic tumour suppressors (e.g. TP53) but not to oncogenes, which typically require only a single activating hit.

entries.label: Cancer Immunosurveillance
entries.hint: The immune system doesn't just fight infection — it continuously patrols the body for cells that have turned cancerous and eliminates most of them before a tumour ever forms. On this view, the cancers we actually see are the rare ones that evolved ways to hide from or disable this surveillance. It reframed cancer as, in part, an immunological failure rather than a purely cellular one.
entries.note: Burnet, F.M. (1970). 'The concept of immunological surveillance.' Progress in Experimental Tumor Research, 13, 1–27 — building on an earlier idea sketched by Lewis Thomas in 1959. The theory fell out of favour in the 1970s when early immunodeficient (nude) mice showed no major increase in spontaneous tumours, but was substantially rehabilitated from the 1990s onward with better mouse models and the discovery of tumour-infiltrating lymphocytes; it is now the theoretical basis for cancer immunotherapy.

entries.label: The Hygiene Hypothesis
entries.hint: Children raised with less exposure to microbes, older siblings, and everyday dirt grow up with higher rates of allergic and autoimmune disease, because their immune systems miss developmental exposures needed to calibrate normal tolerance. David Strachan proposed this after finding hay fever was rarer in children from larger families. It turned rising allergy rates in wealthy countries into evidence about immune development rather than just genetics.
entries.note: Strachan, D.P. (1989). 'Hay fever, hygiene, and household size.' BMJ, 299(6710), 1259–1260. The original "fewer infections" framing has been substantially revised: the strongest current evidence points to reduced exposure to harmless environmental and commensal microbes (the "old friends" hypothesis), not fewer childhood infections specifically, as the operative mechanism — infections themselves can worsen allergic disease.

entries.label: The Phagocytic Theory of Immunity
entries.hint: Specialised mobile cells inside the body actively hunt down, engulf, and digest invading microbes — this is the immune system's front line, not a passive chemical reaction. Ilya Metchnikoff reached this conclusion after watching mobile cells in a transparent starfish larva swarm around a splinter he had inserted, and named the process phagocytosis. It was the founding claim of cellular, as opposed to purely chemical (humoral), immunology.
entries.note: Metchnikoff, E. (1883). 'Untersuchungen über die intracellulare Verdauung bei wirbellosen Thieren.' Arbeiten aus dem Zoologischen Institute der Universität Wien, 5, 141–168. Metchnikoff shared the 1908 Nobel Prize with Paul Ehrlich, whose rival humoral (antibody-based) theory of immunity turned out to be equally correct — the two mechanisms are now understood as complementary arms (innate cellular vs. adaptive humoral) of one immune system, not competitors.

entries.label: Pattern Recognition Theory
entries.hint: The innate immune system doesn't recognise specific pathogens the way antibodies do — it recognises broad, ancient molecular patterns shared by whole classes of microbes (like bacterial cell-wall components) using a small, fixed set of receptors. Charles Janeway proposed this to explain why the innate immune system reacts so fast without needing to "learn" anything, and predicted the existence of the receptors years before any were found.
entries.note: Janeway, C.A. Jr. (1989). 'Approaching the asymptote? Evolution and revolution in immunology.' Cold Spring Harbor Symposia on Quantitative Biology, 54, 1–13. Vindicated when Toll-like receptors were identified as exactly this kind of pattern-recognition receptor — first in Drosophila (Lemaitre et al., 1996) and then in mammals (Medzhitov, Preston-Hurlburt & Janeway, 1997; Poltorak et al., 1998) — work that won Beutler and Hoffmann a share of the 2011 Nobel Prize.

categories.label: Landmark Studies
categories.subtitle: The defining experiments — and exactly what they found

entries.label: Jenner's Smallpox Vaccination
entries.hint: Edward Jenner inoculated an eight-year-old boy, James Phipps, with matter from a cowpox lesion, then deliberately exposed him to smallpox — the boy stayed healthy. Milkmaids who had caught cowpox were known locally to be immune to smallpox; Jenner turned that folk observation into a controlled, repeatable procedure, coining the word "vaccine" from vacca, Latin for cow.
entries.note: Jenner, E. (1798). An Inquiry into the Causes and Effects of the Variolae Vaccinae. London: Sampson Low. The original demonstration was a single patient and would not meet modern experimental standards, but the underlying finding — cross-immunity between cowpox and smallpox — was validated by two more centuries of vaccination and by smallpox's eradication in 1980, the only human disease eliminated this way to date.

entries.label: Koch's Isolation of Mycobacterium tuberculosis
entries.hint: Robert Koch identified, stained, cultured, and reintroduced the tuberculosis bacillus into animals, satisfying his own postulates and proving that a single specific microorganism — not miasma, heredity, or bad air — caused the disease responsible for roughly one in seven deaths in Europe at the time. He announced it to the Berlin Physiological Society on 24 March 1882.
entries.note: Koch, R. (1882). 'Die Aetiologie der Tuberkulose.' Berliner Klinische Wochenschrift, 19, 221–230. One of the most thoroughly validated findings in the history of medicine; Mycobacterium tuberculosis remains the confirmed causative agent, and the discovery directly enabled the century-long public health effort against TB that continues today.

entries.label: The British Doctors Study
entries.hint: Richard Doll and Austin Bradford Hill first linked smoking to lung cancer in a 1950 case-control study of London hospital patients, then confirmed it by prospectively following the smoking habits and death rates of over 40,000 British doctors for decades. It became the template for how epidemiology proves that something causes cancer in humans, where you can't ethically run the equivalent lab experiment.
entries.note: Doll, R., Hill, A.B. (1950). 'Smoking and carcinoma of the lung.' British Medical Journal, 2(4682), 739–748; later results in Doll, R., Peto, R., et al. (2004). 'Mortality in relation to smoking: 50 years' observations on male British doctors.' BMJ, 328(7455), 1519. Among the most robust causal findings in epidemiology — replicated across dozens of countries and populations, with a clear dose-response relationship and reversal of risk on cessation.

entries.label: Warburg's Aerobic Glycolysis Experiments
entries.hint: Otto Warburg found that cancer cells generate most of their energy by fermenting glucose into lactate even when plenty of oxygen is available to run the far more efficient process of respiration — a wasteful-looking strategy healthy cells only use when starved of oxygen. This became known as the Warburg effect, and it's now exploited directly: PET scans detect tumours by imaging exactly this excess glucose uptake.
entries.note: Warburg, O. (1924). 'Über den Stoffwechsel der Carcinomzelle.' Biochemische Zeitschrift, 152, 309–344; summarised in English in Warburg, O. (1956). 'On the origin of cancer cells.' Science, 123(3191), 309–314. The metabolic observation is robust and extensively replicated. Warburg's own causal explanation — that irreversible damage to mitochondrial respiration is the primary cause of cancer — has been largely superseded: aerobic glycolysis is now understood mostly as a downstream consequence of oncogene activation (MYC, PI3K/AKT, HIF-1) rather than the root cause, and whether it is a cause or a consequence of malignancy remains actively debated.

entries.label: Establishment of the HeLa Cell Line
entries.hint: George Gey's lab successfully grew the first human cell line that would divide indefinitely outside the body, derived from a cervical cancer biopsy taken from a patient named Henrietta Lacks without her knowledge or consent. HeLa cells went on to enable the development of the polio vaccine, in vitro fertilisation techniques, and much of modern cell biology — a single unauthorised biopsy underwrites an enormous share of 20th-century medicine.
entries.note: Gey, G.O., Coffman, W.D., Kubicek, M.T. (1952). 'Tissue culture studies of the proliferative capacity of cervical carcinoma and normal epithelium.' Cancer Research, 12, 264–265. The immortality and research utility of the line is beyond dispute after 70+ years of continuous use; the serious ethical controversy over consent, privacy, and commercial use of Lacks's cells without her family's knowledge (only settled in part by a 2013 NIH agreement with her descendants) is a landmark case in research ethics.

entries.label: Marshall's Helicobacter pylori Self-Experiment
entries.hint: Barry Marshall drank a Petri dish culture of Helicobacter pylori and developed acute gastritis days later, deliberately fulfilling Koch's postulates on himself after his and Robin Warren's bacterial theory of ulcers was widely dismissed — the medical consensus of the time held that ulcers were caused by stress and stomach acid alone. The stunt, combined with the underlying research, overturned decades of dogma and made most peptic ulcers a curable bacterial infection instead of a chronic condition managed with acid suppressants.
entries.note: Marshall, B.J., Warren, J.R. (1984). 'Unidentified curved bacilli in the stomach of patients with gastritis and peptic ulceration.' The Lancet, 323(8390), 1311–1315; self-experiment reported in Marshall, B.J., Armstrong, J.A., McGechie, D.B., Glancy, R.J. (1985). 'Attempt to fulfil Koch's postulates for pyloric Campylobacter.' Medical Journal of Australia, 142(8), 436–439. Robustly confirmed and clinically transformative — Marshall and Warren shared the 2005 Nobel Prize; antibiotic treatment of H. pylori is now standard of care for most peptic ulcer disease.

entries.label: Zinkernagel and Doherty's MHC Restriction Experiments
entries.hint: Killer T cells from a virus-infected mouse could destroy infected cells from a genetically identical mouse, but not equally infected cells from a different mouse strain — meaning T cells don't just recognise the virus, they recognise virus plus the body's own "self" tissue-marker molecules (MHC) at the same time. Rolf Zinkernagel and Peter Doherty's mouse experiments revealed the basic grammar of how the adaptive immune system reads infected cells, a mechanism nobody had predicted.
entries.note: Zinkernagel, R.M., Doherty, P.C. (1974). 'Restriction of in vitro T cell-mediated cytotoxicity in lymphocytic choriomeningitis within a syngeneic or semiallogeneic system.' Nature, 248(5450), 701–702. Foundational and unchallenged since; it won the 1996 Nobel Prize and underlies the entire modern understanding of antigen presentation, transplant rejection, and T-cell vaccine design.

entries.label: Discovery of HIV
entries.hint: Françoise Barré-Sinoussi and Luc Montagnier isolated a previously unknown retrovirus, initially named LAV, from the lymph node of a patient at risk of the newly described disease AIDS — the first hard evidence for what would soon be confirmed as the virus that causes it. Identifying the pathogen a mere two years after AIDS was first clinically recognised opened the door to a blood test, and eventually to antiretroviral treatment.
entries.note: Barré-Sinoussi, F., Chermann, J.C., Rey, F., et al. (1983). 'Isolation of a T-lymphotropic retrovirus from a patient at risk for acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS).' Science, 220(4599), 868–871. Robustly confirmed; Barré-Sinoussi and Montagnier received the 2008 Nobel Prize. A bitter, well-documented priority dispute with Robert Gallo's NIH laboratory over discovery credit and the HIV blood test patent ran through the 1980s and was only diplomatically resolved by a 1987 Reagan–Chirac agreement to share royalties.

entries.label: Rosenberg's IL-2 and Adoptive Cell Therapy Trials
entries.hint: Steven Rosenberg's team treated patients with advanced, otherwise untreatable metastatic cancer using their own immune cells, grown and activated outside the body with the T-cell growth factor interleukin-2, then reinfused. Some patients — including a minority with melanoma and kidney cancer — had dramatic, durable tumour regressions, becoming the first solid proof that a person's own immune system could be deliberately weaponised against their cancer.
entries.note: Rosenberg, S.A., Lotze, M.T., Muul, L.M., et al. (1985). 'Observations on the systemic administration of autologous lymphokine-activated killer cells and recombinant interleukin-2 to patients with metastatic cancer.' New England Journal of Medicine, 313(23), 1485–1492. Response rates were real but modest and inconsistent (roughly 15–20% of patients, concentrated in melanoma and renal cell carcinoma), with severe treatment toxicity from high-dose IL-2 — later superseded and complemented by checkpoint inhibitors and engineered CAR-T cells, but this was the proof of principle that made those approaches worth pursuing.

entries.label: First Pediatric CAR-T Trial for Leukemia
entries.hint: A seven-year-old girl with relapsed, chemotherapy-resistant leukemia was infused with her own T cells, genetically engineered to carry a chimeric antigen receptor targeting the protein CD19 on her cancer cells. She went into complete remission within a month, becoming the first pediatric patient to demonstrate that CAR-T cell therapy could work — a treatment now approved and used worldwide for relapsed B-cell leukemias.
entries.note: Grupp, S.A., Kalos, M., Barrett, D., et al. (2013). 'Chimeric antigen receptor–modified T cells for acute lymphoid leukemia.' New England Journal of Medicine, 368(16), 1509–1518. Widely replicated — CAR-T therapy (tisagenlecleucel, brand name Kymriah) received FDA approval in 2017 and is now standard for relapsed/refractory B-cell ALL. Serious caveats remain: cytokine release syndrome and neurotoxicity are common acute risks, and CAR-T has so far shown far less success against solid tumours than blood cancers.

entries.label: CTLA-4 Blockade Experiments
entries.hint: James Allison's lab showed that blocking a single molecule, CTLA-4, which normally acts as a brake on T cells, was enough to make a mouse's own immune system attack and shrink established tumours. The insight — that the real obstacle to fighting cancer is often not a weak immune system but an immune system held back by its own checkpoints — created an entirely new drug category, checkpoint inhibitors, built on releasing the brake rather than pressing the accelerator.
entries.note: Leach, D.R., Krummel, M.F., Allison, J.P. (1996). 'Enhancement of antitumor immunity by CTLA-4 blockade.' Science, 271(5256), 1734–1736. Directly led to ipilimumab, the first checkpoint inhibitor approved by the FDA (2011, for melanoma); Allison shared the 2018 Nobel Prize with Tasuku Honjo, whose parallel discovery of the PD-1 checkpoint led to a second major drug class. Response is far from universal — many tumour types show little benefit, and predicting which patients will respond remains an open clinical problem.

categories.label: Effects & Phenomena
categories.subtitle: Named, replicable patterns in cellular injury, immune response, and disease progression

entries.label: The Warburg Effect
entries.hint: Cancer cells across nearly every tumour type consistently burn glucose the wasteful way — fermenting it to lactate — even when there's ample oxygen to run efficient respiration instead. It's now understood as a deliberate metabolic strategy that supplies fast-dividing cells with the raw building blocks for new DNA, membranes and proteins, not evidence of broken mitochondria as Warburg first thought. It is also directly exploited in the clinic: FDG-PET cancer scans work by imaging exactly this excess glucose uptake.
entries.note: Vander Heiden, M.G., Cantley, L.C., Thompson, C.B. (2009). 'Understanding the Warburg Effect: The Metabolic Requirements of Cell Proliferation.' Science, 324(5930), 1029–1033 — the modern molecular reframing, building on Warburg's original 1924 observation (see st4). The phenomenon itself is extremely robust and clinically load-bearing (PET imaging); the open question is not whether it happens but why it's selected for — the leading view is that it trades energy efficiency for biosynthetic speed, driven by oncogenes like MYC and HIF-1, not the mitochondrial damage Warburg proposed.

entries.label: Cytokine Storm
entries.hint: A runaway immune response — cytokine signals amplifying more cytokine release in a feedback loop — can cause severe, sometimes fatal, damage to the very body the immune system is meant to protect. It shows up wherever a very fast, very strong immune activation occurs: severe sepsis, some viral infections, and as an on-target side effect of powerful cancer immunotherapies like CAR-T cells and checkpoint inhibitors.
entries.note: Lee, D.W., Santomasso, B.D., Locke, F.L., et al. (2019). 'ASTCT Consensus Grading for Cytokine Release Syndrome and Neurologic Toxicity Associated with Immune Effector Cells.' Biology of Blood and Marrow Transplantation, 25(4), 625–638 — the modern clinical grading standard, developed largely in response to CAR-T toxicity (see st10). The underlying biology was described earlier (Tisoncik, J.R., et al., 2012, 'Into the Eye of the Cytokine Storm,' Microbiology and Molecular Biology Reviews, 76(1), 16–32); severity thresholds and optimal management (e.g. tocilizumab timing) are still actively refined.

entries.label: Antigenic Drift and Shift
entries.hint: Influenza evades immunity two different ways: slow, gradual mutation of its surface proteins (drift) erodes existing immunity year over year, which is why flu vaccines need reformulating annually, while an abrupt reassortment of entire gene segments between strains (shift) can create a virus nobody has any immunity to at all — the mechanism behind pandemic flu. The distinction explains both routine seasonal flu and rare pandemic flu with one underlying model.
entries.note: Webster, R.G., Bean, W.J., Gorman, O.T., Chambers, T.M., Kawaoka, Y. (1992). 'Evolution and ecology of influenza A viruses.' Microbiological Reviews, 56(1), 152–179. Extensively validated across decades of global influenza surveillance; the model correctly predicted that pandemic strains (1957, 1968, 2009) arise from reassortment events, often involving animal reservoirs.

entries.label: Molecular Mimicry
entries.hint: Sometimes a pathogen's proteins happen to resemble the body's own tissue closely enough that antibodies or T cells trained to attack the pathogen mistakenly attack the host too. The clearest confirmed case is rheumatic fever: antibodies against Group A Streptococcus cross-react with heart valve tissue, causing lasting cardiac damage weeks after a simple strep throat has cleared.
entries.note: Cunningham, M.W. (2000). 'Pathogenesis of Group A Streptococcal Infections.' Clinical Microbiology Reviews, 13(3), 470–511. The rheumatic fever mechanism is robustly established. As a general explanation extended to other autoimmune diseases (e.g. some proposed viral triggers of type 1 diabetes or multiple sclerosis), the evidence is considerably more circumstantial — cross-reactivity in a test tube doesn't prove it drives disease in a patient, so treat mimicry outside rheumatic fever as a plausible but not fully proven mechanism.

entries.label: Tumour Immune Evasion
entries.hint: Tumours that survive long enough to be diagnosed have usually done more than out-grow the body's defences — many actively sabotage them, for instance by displaying PD-L1, a molecule that engages the immune system's own "stand down" checkpoint on T cells. This single mechanistic insight — that cancer can hijack a normal safety brake — is the entire rationale behind checkpoint inhibitor drugs.
entries.note: Dunn, G.P., Old, L.J., Schreiber, R.D. (2004). 'The Three Es of Cancer Immunoediting.' Annual Review of Immunology, 22, 329–360 — describing Elimination, Equilibrium, and Escape as the three phases of the tumour-immune relationship, with evasion (Escape) as the final stage. Robustly confirmed and directly clinically actionable (see st11); different tumours escape by different routes, so checkpoint blockade alone doesn't work for every cancer type.

entries.label: Graft-Versus-Host Disease
entries.hint: When a patient receives a bone marrow or stem cell transplant from a donor, the transplanted immune cells can recognise the recipient's own body as foreign and attack it — the transplant attacking the patient, rather than the more familiar reverse. Rupert Billingham identified the three conditions required for this to happen: the graft must contain immune-competent cells, the recipient must express antigens the graft doesn't recognise as self, and the recipient must be unable to reject the graft first.
entries.note: Billingham, R.E. (1966). 'The biology of graft-versus-host reactions.' Harvey Lectures, 62, 21–78. Extremely well characterised clinically — GvHD remains a leading cause of transplant-related complications and mortality, managed today with immunosuppression and careful donor HLA matching; Billingham's own earlier tolerance work (see th4) makes this an unusually direct throughline from basic science to clinical hazard.

entries.label: Herd Immunity
entries.hint: Once enough of a population is immune to a disease, transmission chains break down even for the people who aren't immune — a kind of indirect protection that let vaccination campaigns eliminate diseases like smallpox without needing to vaccinate literally everyone. How high that threshold needs to be depends entirely on how contagious the specific disease is.
entries.note: Fine, P.E.M. (1993). 'Herd Immunity: History, Theory, Practice.' Epidemiologic Reviews, 15(2), 265–302. Robustly validated across numerous vaccination campaigns (measles, smallpox eradication in 1980). The precise threshold is disease-specific and was a genuine point of public confusion and political contention during COVID-19, where waning immunity and viral evolution complicated simple threshold estimates.

entries.label: Immunological Memory
entries.hint: A second exposure to the same antigen produces a faster, larger, and higher-affinity immune response than the first ever did, because the initial exposure leaves behind long-lived memory B and T cells primed to respond immediately. This is the entire logic of vaccine booster doses, and it's why some infections (measles, chickenpox) confer lifelong protection after a single encounter while others don't.
entries.note: Ahmed, R., Gray, D. (1996). 'Immunological Memory and Protective Immunity: Understanding Their Relation.' Science, 272(5258), 54–60. A foundational, well-replicated concept, though the durability of memory varies enormously by pathogen and vaccine type — some memory responses last decades, others (like influenza and some coronaviruses) wane within a year or two, a distinction with direct implications for booster scheduling.

entries.label: Loss of Contact Inhibition in Malignant Transformation
entries.hint: Normal cells stop dividing once they're surrounded by neighbours on all sides — a built-in brake called contact inhibition. Cancer cells lose this brake and keep piling on top of each other and dividing regardless of crowding, which is exactly what a petri dish of transformed cells looks like under a microscope: a normal culture stops at a single flat layer, a malignant one keeps growing into disorganised heaps.
entries.note: Abercrombie, M. (1979). 'Contact inhibition and malignancy.' Nature, 281(5729), 259–262. A classic, robustly reproduced in vitro hallmark of transformation, still used today as a simple functional assay of malignant potential; the molecular mechanism (loss of contact-dependent signalling through proteins like E-cadherin and Merlin/NF2) was worked out decades later.

entries.label: Antibiotic Resistance
entries.hint: Bacterial populations exposed to an antibiotic rapidly evolve resistance to it, through selection of pre-existing resistant variants or new mutations, often exchanging resistance genes between species entirely. It is ordinary natural selection playing out on a timescale of years rather than millennia, and it means every antibiotic humanity has ever deployed has a resistant strain emerging somewhere in response.
entries.note: Davies, J., Davies, D. (2010). 'Origins and Evolution of Antibiotic Resistance.' Microbiology and Molecular Biology Reviews, 74(3), 417–433. One of the most robustly documented phenomena in biology — resistance genes have even been found in bacteria from environments untouched by human antibiotics, showing the underlying resistance mechanisms substantially predate clinical antibiotic use. Now a recognised global public health crisis (WHO priority pathogen lists).

categories.label: Key Concepts
categories.subtitle: The building blocks and vocabulary every student must command

entries.label: Antigen and Epitope
entries.hint: An antigen is any molecule that can trigger an immune response; an epitope is the specific small patch on that molecule that an antibody or T-cell receptor physically binds. A single large antigen — a whole virus, say — typically carries many different epitopes, which is why the immune response to one pathogen is really many independent responses happening at once.
entries.note: Landsteiner, K. (1936). The Specificity of Serological Reactions. Springfield: Charles C. Thomas. Landsteiner's meticulous immunochemistry (he also discovered the ABO blood groups) established that antibody specificity is exquisitely fine-grained — down to single chemical substitutions — a finding that still anchors how antigen specificity is taught.

entries.label: Opsonization
entries.hint: Coating a pathogen with antibodies or complement proteins makes it dramatically easier for phagocytes to recognise, grab, and engulf it — like seasoning a target to make it appetising. Almroth Wright named these coating molecules "opsonins" (from the Greek for "to prepare food for"), and the concept became the conceptual bridge connecting humoral immunity (antibodies) to cellular immunity (phagocytosis).
entries.note: Wright, A.E., Douglas, S.R. (1903). 'An Experimental Investigation of the Role of the Blood Fluids in Connection with Phagocytosis.' Proceedings of the Royal Society, 72, 357–370. Wright's broader claims about measuring a patient's "opsonic index" for clinical diagnosis did not hold up and were satirised by George Bernard Shaw; the core opsonization concept itself, stripped of that clinical overreach, remains entirely standard immunology.

entries.label: Central vs. Peripheral Tolerance
entries.hint: The immune system polices itself in two stages: central tolerance deletes most self-reactive T and B cells during their development in the thymus and bone marrow, and peripheral tolerance — including a dedicated cell type, regulatory T cells — mops up whatever self-reactive cells slip through afterward. Autoimmune disease can arise from a failure at either stage, which is why the concept matters beyond pure theory.
entries.note: Sakaguchi, S., Sakaguchi, N., Asano, M., Itoh, M., Toda, M. (1995). 'Immunologic Self-Tolerance Maintained by Activated T Cells Expressing IL-2 Receptor Alpha-Chains (CD25).' Journal of Immunology, 155(3), 1151–1164 — the paper that put regulatory T cells (Tregs) on firm experimental footing, decisively confirming the peripheral half of this framework. The central/peripheral distinction itself is now standard, textbook immunology.

entries.label: Tumour Angiogenesis
entries.hint: A tumour can't grow past a couple of millimetres on diffusion alone — it needs its own blood supply, so it actively secretes signals that recruit new blood vessels to grow toward it. Judah Folkman proposed that cutting off this blood supply, rather than attacking the tumour cells directly, could be a viable cancer treatment strategy — an idea that took over twenty years to yield an approved drug, but eventually did.
entries.note: Folkman, J. (1971). 'Tumor Angiogenesis: Therapeutic Implications.' New England Journal of Medicine, 285(21), 1182–1186. Validated by the 2004 FDA approval of bevacizumab (Avastin), an anti-VEGF antibody; anti-angiogenic therapy has proven more useful in combination with other treatments than as a standalone cure, and tumours frequently evolve resistance to it.

entries.label: The Metastatic Cascade
entries.hint: Cancer doesn't spread by accident — it follows a specific multi-step sequence: local invasion into surrounding tissue, entry into blood or lymph vessels, survival while circulating, exit into a new tissue, and finally successful growth in that unfamiliar environment. Most cancer cells that enter circulation fail at one of these steps, which is why metastasis, though the leading cause of cancer death, is actually a very inefficient process from the tumour's perspective.
entries.note: Paget, S. (1889). 'The Distribution of Secondary Growths in Cancer of the Breast.' The Lancet, 133(3421), 571–573 — the original "seed and soil" hypothesis, proposing metastasis depends on compatibility between cancer cells ("seeds") and the target organ environment ("soil"), not just anatomical proximity. Revisited and substantially confirmed at the molecular level over a century later (Fidler, I.J., 2003, 'The Pathogenesis of Cancer Metastasis: The Seed and Soil Hypothesis Revisited,' Nature Reviews Cancer, 3(6), 453–458).

entries.label: Chemokines
entries.hint: Chemokines are a specific family of small signalling proteins whose job is to tell immune cells which direction to move in — laying down a chemical trail that draws phagocytes and lymphocytes toward an infection or, less helpfully, that tumours can hijack to attract cells that help them grow. They're a functionally distinct subset of the broader category of cytokines, immune cell-to-cell signals in general.
entries.note: Zlotnik, A., Yoshie, O. (2000). 'Chemokines: A New Classification System and Their Role in Immunity.' Immunity, 12(2), 121–127 — established the modern CXC/CC/C/CX3C structural classification still used today. The system has needed only minor extension since, as new chemokines and their receptors continue to be characterised.

entries.label: MHC Class I vs. Class II
entries.hint: Nearly every cell in the body displays MHC Class I molecules carrying fragments of its own internal proteins, letting killer T cells check whether a cell has been hijacked by a virus from the inside. Specialised immune cells alone display MHC Class II, carrying fragments of material they've swallowed from outside, for helper T cells to inspect. The two-track system lets the adaptive immune system separately monitor "what's wrong inside this cell" and "what's out there in the body."
entries.note: Neefjes, J., Jongsma, M.L.M., Paul, P., Bakke, O. (2011). 'Towards a systems understanding of MHC class I and MHC class II antigen presentation.' Nature Reviews Immunology, 11(12), 823–836. Builds on the MHC restriction phenomenon Zinkernagel and Doherty first demonstrated (see st7, th3); the Class I/II distinction is standard, well-established immunology, foundational to how vaccines and transplant matching are designed.

entries.label: The Gell–Coombs Classification
entries.hint: Not all allergic or immune-mediated tissue damage works the same way — Philip Gell and Robin Coombs sorted hypersensitivity reactions into four mechanistic types: Type I is immediate IgE-mediated allergy (hay fever, anaphylaxis), Type II is antibody attacking a specific cell surface (some transfusion reactions), Type III is immune complexes depositing in tissue (some forms of lupus), and Type IV is delayed T-cell-mediated reaction (contact dermatitis, tuberculin skin tests). The scheme is still the standard way to teach why allergic and autoimmune reactions look so different from each other.
entries.note: Gell, P.G.H., Coombs, R.R.A. (1963). Clinical Aspects of Immunology. Oxford: Blackwell Scientific Publications. A classification system, not a falsifiable hypothesis, so it isn't "replicated" in the usual sense — but it has proven durable enough that it's still the standard teaching framework six decades later, with only minor proposed extensions (e.g. a sometimes-cited Type V for stimulatory autoantibodies).

categories.label: Methods & Approaches
categories.subtitle: The tools and paradigms that generate the field's evidence

entries.label: ELISA
entries.hint: A way to detect and measure a specific antibody or antigen in a fluid sample using an enzyme that produces a visible colour change when the target is present — the intensity of colour tells you roughly how much is there. It replaced slower, more hazardous radioactive-label assays and became the workhorse behind most diagnostic blood tests, from HIV screening to allergy panels to pregnancy tests.
entries.note: Engvall, E., Perlmann, P. (1971). 'Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). Quantitative assay of immunoglobulin G.' Immunochemistry, 8(9), 871–874. Still the diagnostic-immunology default fifty years on; countless clinical variants (sandwich ELISA, competitive ELISA) have been built on the same enzyme-label principle.

entries.label: Flow Cytometry
entries.hint: A method for passing individual cells one at a time past a laser, reading which fluorescently-tagged antibodies each cell has bound on its surface, and sorting millions of cells per hour by exactly which combination of markers they carry. It's how a T-cell count is measured, how immune cell subtypes are distinguished from each other in the first place, and how a specific rare cell population can be physically isolated for further study.
entries.note: Julius, M.H., Masuda, T., Herzenberg, L.A. (1972). 'Demonstration that antigen-binding cells are precursors of antibody-producing cells after purification with a fluorescence-activated cell sorter.' Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 69(7), 1934–1938 — one of Leonard Herzenberg's foundational FACS papers. Modern instruments can now read 30+ markers on a single cell simultaneously, far beyond what the original method could resolve.

entries.label: Polymerase Chain Reaction for Pathogen Detection
entries.hint: A way to take a trace amount of a pathogen's genetic material — sometimes just a handful of molecules — and repeatedly copy it until there's enough to reliably detect, using a heat-tolerant enzyme that survives the repeated heating and cooling cycles the copying process requires. It turned pathogen diagnosis from "culture it and wait days" into "amplify and detect in hours," and became a household term during COVID-19 as the basis of the standard PCR test.
entries.note: Saiki, R.K., Gelfand, D.H., Stoffel, S., et al. (1988). 'Primer-Directed Enzymatic Amplification of DNA with a Thermostable DNA Polymerase.' Science, 239(4839), 487–491 — introducing Taq polymerase, which made Kary Mullis's original 1985 PCR concept practical to automate. Mullis received the 1993 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for the underlying idea.

entries.label: Histopathological Staining
entries.hint: Cutting tissue into slices thin enough for light to pass through and staining them with dyes that colour different cell structures differently — classically haematoxylin (stains nuclei blue-purple) paired with eosin (stains cytoplasm and protein pink) — lets a pathologist read a tissue's story under a microscope. It remains the single most-performed diagnostic test in cancer medicine: nearly every cancer diagnosis still starts with a biopsy read this way.
entries.note: The haematoxylin/eosin (H&E) combination developed incrementally through the 1860s–1870s via multiple European histologists rather than one landmark paper; see Titford, M. (2005). 'The Long History of Hematoxylin.' Biotechnic & Histochemistry, 80(2), 73–78 for the consolidated history. Despite over a century of alternative and molecular diagnostic techniques, H&E staining remains the irreplaceable first step in most cancer diagnoses.

entries.label: Monoclonal Antibody Production (Hybridoma Technique)
entries.hint: Fusing a single antibody-producing immune cell with an immortal cancer cell creates a hybrid cell — a hybridoma — that combines the immune cell's ability to make one specific, pure antibody with the cancer cell's ability to divide forever. This single trick made it possible to manufacture unlimited quantities of any desired antibody, and underlies most modern antibody-based drugs, from checkpoint inhibitors to countless diagnostic tests.
entries.note: Köhler, G., Milstein, C. (1975). 'Continuous cultures of fused cells secreting antibody of predefined specificity.' Nature, 256(5517), 495–497. Köhler and Milstein shared the 1984 Nobel Prize; notably, they deliberately did not patent the technique, a decision later widely debated given the enormous commercial value monoclonal antibody drugs went on to generate.

entries.label: Knockout Mouse Models
entries.hint: Precisely disabling a single gene in a living mouse — and breeding a strain that inherits the disabled version — lets researchers directly observe what that gene actually does by watching what goes wrong without it. For immunology specifically, it has been the primary tool for proving that a given receptor, cytokine, or signalling protein is genuinely necessary for a specific immune function, rather than just associated with it.
entries.note: Thomas, K.R., Capecchi, M.R. (1987). 'Site-directed mutagenesis by gene targeting in mouse embryo-derived stem cells.' Cell, 51(3), 503–512. Capecchi shared the 2007 Nobel Prize with Martin Evans and Oliver Smithies for developing gene-targeting technology; newer CRISPR-based methods have since made knockout generation dramatically faster, though the underlying logic of the experiment is unchanged.

entries.label: Tumour Xenograft Models
entries.hint: Implanting a piece of human tumour directly into a mouse that lacks the immune system components needed to reject it lets that human tumour keep growing in a living animal, where its response to a candidate drug can be tested before ever giving the drug to a person. It became possible only once genetically immunodeficient ("nude") mice were available to accept a human graft without rejecting it as foreign.
entries.note: Rygaard, J., Povlsen, C.O. (1969). 'Heterotransplantation of a human malignant tumour to "Nude" mice.' Acta Pathologica et Microbiologica Scandinavica, 77(4), 758–760. Now a standard preclinical step in cancer drug development; a significant limitation is that these mice's compromised immune systems mean the models can't capture how a fully intact immune system would interact with the tumour or a candidate immunotherapy.

categories.label: Pioneers & Theorists
categories.subtitle: The researchers who shaped the discipline

entries.label: Rudolf Virchow
entries.hint: Established that disease originates in disturbances at the level of the individual cell, not from imbalances in bodily fluids as ancient and medieval medicine had held — the single idea that founded modern pathology as a discipline. He also contributed the germ-line observation "omnis cellula e cellula," recognised leukaemia as a distinct disease, and was a pioneering public-health reformer in Berlin.
entries.note: Die Cellularpathologie (1858); also a prolific public health campaigner (sanitation, hospital reform) and an early advocate against "race science," which he publicly and scientifically opposed. Sometimes called "the father of modern pathology."

entries.label: Robert Koch
entries.hint: Proved that specific microorganisms cause specific diseases by isolating Bacillus anthracis (anthrax, 1876) and Mycobacterium tuberculosis (1882), then formalising the four-part logical standard — Koch's postulates — still taught as the baseline for proving microbial causation. Tuberculosis alone was killing roughly one in seven Europeans at the time of his discovery.
entries.note: 'Die Aetiologie der Tuberkulose' (1882); awarded the 1905 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. His later tuberculin treatment for TB was a well-documented clinical failure, a useful reminder that Koch's own record includes real, disclosed mistakes alongside the foundational successes.

entries.label: Paul Ehrlich
entries.hint: Developed Salvarsan, the first drug designed and systematically screened to kill a specific pathogen (the bacterium causing syphilis) while sparing the patient — the founding demonstration of chemotherapy as a rational discipline, which he called the search for a "magic bullet." He separately proposed an influential (and largely correct) side-chain theory explaining how antibodies bind antigens.
entries.note: Salvarsan announced 1910; shared the 1908 Nobel Prize with Metchnikoff for immunity work. Founded and directed the Georg-Speyer-Haus research institute in Frankfurt, where the Salvarsan screening programme was carried out.

entries.label: Ilya Metchnikoff
entries.hint: Discovered phagocytosis — that specialised mobile cells actively engulf and destroy invading microbes — establishing cellular immunity as a real mechanism distinct from and complementary to antibody-based (humoral) immunity. He reached the insight by watching immune cells swarm a splinter he inserted into a transparent starfish larva under a microscope.
entries.note: 'Untersuchungen über die intracellulare Verdauung bei wirbellosen Thieren' (1883); shared the 1908 Nobel Prize with his scientific rival Paul Ehrlich, whose humoral theory turned out to be equally correct rather than competing.

entries.label: Edward Jenner
entries.hint: Turned the folk observation that milkmaids who caught cowpox seemed immune to smallpox into a deliberate, testable medical procedure, coining the term "vaccine." His single case study eventually led to the only successful human eradication of a disease in history — smallpox was declared eradicated in 1980.
entries.note: An Inquiry into the Causes and Effects of the Variolae Vaccinae (1798). Jenner's original procedure — inoculating with live cowpox matter taken from other patients — would not meet modern safety or consent standards, though the underlying biological principle it demonstrated was entirely sound.

entries.label: Karl Landsteiner
entries.hint: Discovered the ABO blood group system, explaining why some blood transfusions caused fatal reactions while others didn't — a single finding that made transfusion medicine safe almost overnight. He later carried out equally foundational immunochemistry establishing just how precisely antibodies discriminate between molecular shapes.
entries.note: ABO blood groups described 1901 (Nobel Prize, 1930); The Specificity of Serological Reactions (1936) — this later immunochemical work is cited directly in this folio (see co1). Also co-discovered the Rh blood group factor in 1940, late in his career.

entries.label: Macfarlane Burnet
entries.hint: Proposed clonal selection theory — that each immune cell is pre-committed to one antigen shape before it ever meets it, and proliferates only once selected by a match — replacing the older idea that antigens physically shape antibodies. He also theoretically predicted that immune tolerance could be actively acquired during development, a prediction experimentally confirmed by Medawar's team.
entries.note: 'A modification of Jerne's theory of antibody production using the concept of clonal selection' (1957); shared the 1960 Nobel Prize with Medawar for the tolerance work. Also proposed cancer immunosurveillance (see th7), a theory that fell out of favour for two decades before being substantially rehabilitated.

entries.label: Peter Medawar
entries.hint: Experimentally proved that immune self/non-self discrimination is learned during development rather than fixed at birth, by showing that mice exposed to foreign cells before birth grow up permanently tolerant of tissue from that same donor. The finding gave transplant medicine its first theoretical foundation — until then, there was no scientific reason to think immune rejection could ever be reliably overcome.
entries.note: Billingham, Brent, Medawar, 'Actively acquired tolerance of foreign cells' (1953); shared the 1960 Nobel Prize with Burnet. Medawar's wartime work on skin grafting for burns victims directly motivated his later transplant-immunology research.

entries.label: Otto Warburg
entries.hint: Discovered that cancer cells consistently generate energy through wasteful glucose fermentation even when oxygen is plentiful — the phenomenon that bears his name — and spent much of his career arguing (incorrectly, as later work showed) that this reflected irreversible damage to cellular respiration and was the primary cause of cancer itself. The observation long outlived his causal theory and is now central to how cancer is imaged and understood metabolically.
entries.note: 'Über den Stoffwechsel der Carcinomzelle' (1924); Nobel Prize 1931 for an earlier, separate discovery (the enzyme of cellular respiration). His causal theory of cancer's origin was a significant scientific overreach that the field spent decades correcting — a useful case study in distinguishing a robust observation from its original, since-revised explanation.

entries.label: James Allison
entries.hint: Showed that blocking a single molecular brake on T cells, CTLA-4, was enough to make a mouse's immune system destroy its own tumours — establishing that cancer immunotherapy could work by releasing an existing immune response rather than trying to boost a weak one. The insight founded the entire checkpoint inhibitor drug class.
entries.note: Leach, Krummel, Allison, 'Enhancement of Antitumor Immunity by CTLA-4 Blockade' (1996); shared the 2018 Nobel Prize with Tasuku Honjo. His CTLA-4 antibody became ipilimumab, the first FDA-approved checkpoint inhibitor (2011).

entries.label: Tasuku Honjo
entries.hint: Discovered PD-1, a second and independent immune checkpoint molecule distinct from CTLA-4, initially while studying genes involved in programmed cell death. Drugs blocking PD-1 (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) have since become the most widely used class of cancer immunotherapy, often better tolerated than CTLA-4 blockade.
entries.note: Ishida, Y., Agata, Y., Shibahara, K., Honjo, T. (1992). 'Induced expression of PD-1, a novel member of the immunoglobulin gene superfamily, upon programmed cell death.' The EMBO Journal, 11(11), 3887–3895. Shared the 2018 Nobel Prize with James Allison; PD-1/PD-L1 blockade is now backbone therapy across dozens of cancer types.

entries.label: Barry Marshall
entries.hint: Proved that a bacterium, not stress or stomach acid, causes most peptic ulcers — and when the medical establishment wouldn't believe him, drank a culture of the bacterium himself to develop gastritis on demand and settle the argument. The discovery turned a chronic, acid-suppressant-managed condition into a bacterial infection curable with a course of antibiotics.
entries.note: Marshall, Warren, 'Unidentified curved bacilli in the stomach of patients with gastritis and peptic ulceration' (1984); self-experiment in Marshall et al., Medical Journal of Australia (1985). Shared the 2005 Nobel Prize with Robin Warren; widely cited as a case study in how entrenched medical consensus can resist strong contrary evidence.

categories.label: Debates & Open Questions
categories.subtitle: Live controversies — where the field is actively contesting the evidence

entries.label: Is the Warburg Effect a Cause or a Consequence of Cancer?
entries.hint: Warburg himself argued that damaged cellular respiration causes cancer, with aerobic glycolysis as the visible symptom. The mainstream modern view inverts this entirely: oncogene activation (MYC, PI3K/AKT, HIF-1) drives the metabolic switch, making it a consequence and enabler of malignancy, not its root cause. A minority of researchers still argue metabolism itself can be the primary driver in at least some cancers — a live, unresolved argument about where in the causal chain metabolic reprogramming actually sits.
entries.note: Mainstream consequence view: DeBerardinis, R.J., Chandel, N.S. (2016). 'Fundamentals of cancer metabolism.' Science Advances, 2(5), e1600200. Minority metabolism-as-driver view: Seyfried, T.N., Shelton, L.M. (2010). 'Cancer as a metabolic disease.' Nutrition & Metabolism, 7, 7 — a real, peer-reviewed but distinctly minority position within cancer biology, not broadly accepted as the primary causal mechanism but a genuine ongoing point of contention rather than settled consensus.

entries.label: Henrietta Lacks and the Ethics of the HeLa Cell Line
entries.hint: HeLa cells were taken from Henrietta Lacks's cervical tumour in 1951 without her knowledge or consent — standard, unremarkable practice under the research norms of the time, but a case that looks starkly different by modern research-ethics standards. Her genome sequence was published without family input as recently as 2013, prompting public objection and forcing a real institutional reckoning about consent, privacy, and who benefits from research built on someone else's tissue.
entries.note: Skloot, R. (2010). The Immortal Life of Henrietta Lacks. New York: Crown Publishers — the book that brought the case to wide public attention. Partial resolution: Hudson, K.L., Collins, F.S. (2013). 'Family Matters — On the Importance of Considering the Family in Research.' New England Journal of Medicine, 369(19), 1780–1781, announcing a 2013 NIH agreement giving the Lacks family a voice in HeLa genome data access — not full retrospective consent or compensation, which remain genuinely contested questions.

entries.label: What Actually Drives the Hygiene Hypothesis?
entries.hint: Strachan's original 1989 framing blamed fewer childhood infections for rising allergic disease. That specific claim hasn't held up well — infections themselves can worsen allergic and autoimmune conditions rather than prevent them. The strongest current evidence instead points to reduced exposure to harmless environmental and commensal microbes shaping immune development, a meaningfully different mechanism now known as the "old friends" hypothesis.
entries.note: Original framing: Strachan, D.P. (1989), see th8. Revised framing: Rook, G.A.W. (2010). 'Hygiene Hypothesis and Autoimmune Diseases.' Clinical Reviews in Allergy & Immunology, 42(1), 5–15, arguing exposure to specific co-evolved microbial and parasitic organisms, not infections generally, calibrates regulatory immune pathways. The public-facing "hygiene hypothesis" label persists even though the mechanism it now describes is substantially different from Strachan's original claim.

entries.label: Does Screening Reduce Cancer Deaths, or Just Find More Cancer?
entries.hint: Randomised screening trials show real mortality reductions for some cancers, which is the entire justification for population screening programmes. Critics argue those benefits are partly an illusion of lead-time bias (finding a cancer earlier doesn't always mean living longer, just knowing longer) and overdiagnosis (detecting slow-growing tumours that would never have caused harm, then treating them anyway). Both effects are real; the argument is over how much of the apparent benefit each explains.
entries.note: Critical position: Welch, H.G., Black, W.C. (2010). 'Overdiagnosis in Cancer.' Journal of the National Cancer Institute, 102(9), 605–613. Balanced trial evidence: Independent UK Panel on Breast Cancer Screening (2012). 'The benefits and harms of breast cancer screening: an independent review.' The Lancet, 380(9855), 1778–1786 — which found a genuine net mortality benefit from mammography while explicitly acknowledging substantial overdiagnosis, illustrating that both sides of this debate are drawing on the same underlying data, not disputing the facts so much as how to weigh them.

entries.label: Is Chronic Inflammation a Cause or a Consequence of Metabolic Disease?
entries.hint: The traditional view treats inflammation in conditions like atherosclerosis and type 2 diabetes as a downstream response to lipid and metabolic dysfunction. A major clinical trial complicated that picture: blocking a single inflammatory cytokine reduced cardiovascular events in patients whose cholesterol was already well controlled, meaning inflammation was doing causal work of its own, not just reacting to underlying lipid disease.
entries.note: Ridker, P.M., Everett, B.M., Thuren, T., et al. (2017). 'Antiinflammatory Therapy with Canakinumab for Atherosclerotic Disease.' New England Journal of Medicine, 377(12), 1119–1131 (the CANTOS trial). The result reopened rather than settled the causal question — canakinumab's benefit was real but modest, and it remains debated how much of cardiometabolic disease is inflammation-driven versus lipid-driven, or whether the two are too intertwined to cleanly separate.

entries.label: Can Cell Therapy Ever Work Against Solid Tumours?
entries.hint: CAR-T therapy transformed treatment of blood cancers, where the target cells circulate freely and are easy for engineered T cells to reach. Solid tumours are a much harder problem: a dense, immunosuppressive tumour microenvironment blocks T-cell infiltration, exhausts the T cells that do get in, and the tumours themselves are more molecularly heterogeneous, so a single target antigen rarely covers the whole tumour. Whether these are solvable engineering problems or a fundamental ceiling on the approach is genuinely unresolved.
entries.note: Martinez, M., Moon, E.K. (2019). 'CAR T Cells for Solid Tumors: New Strategies for Finding, Infiltrating, and Surviving in the Tumor Microenvironment.' Frontiers in Immunology, 10, 128. Early solid-tumour trials (e.g. mesothelin- and GD2-targeted CAR-T) have shown encouraging but so far far more modest and less durable responses than the blood-cancer results (see st10); optimists point to rapid engineering progress, sceptics to the structural mismatch between the therapy and the target.

entries.label: The Autism-Vaccine Myth
entries.hint: A single 1998 paper claiming a link between the MMR vaccine and autism triggered a lasting drop in vaccination rates and multiple subsequent measles outbreaks — despite the paper being retracted, its lead author being struck off the UK medical register for data manipulation and undisclosed financial conflicts of interest, and every large, well-designed follow-up study since finding no association whatsoever between vaccination and autism.
entries.note: Original (retracted) claim: Wakefield, A.J., et al. (1998). 'Ileal-lymphoid-nodular hyperplasia, non-specific colitis, and pervasive developmental disorder in children.' The Lancet, 351(9103), 637–641 — retracted in full by The Lancet in 2010. Definitive rebuttal: Taylor, L.E., Swerdfeger, A.L., Eslick, G.D. (2014). 'Vaccines are not associated with autism: An evidence-based meta-analysis of case-control and cohort studies.' Vaccine, 32(29), 3623–3629, covering over 1.2 million children. Not a live scientific dispute — the science is settled — but a live public-health problem, since the myth continues to suppress vaccination uptake decades after being thoroughly debunked.

entries.label: Has Precision Oncology Delivered on Its Promise?
entries.hint: Matching a drug to a tumour's specific genomic mutations has produced some genuine triumphs — imatinib for BCR-ABL-driven leukaemia turned a fatal disease into a manageable chronic condition. Critics argue those wins are the exception: for most solid tumours, "basket trials" that match patients to targeted drugs by mutation alone, regardless of cancer type, have shown disappointingly low response rates, and the promise of genomic medicine has outpaced what tumour biology has actually delivered for the average patient.
entries.note: Critical position: Prasad, V. (2016). 'Perspective: The precision-oncology illusion.' Nature, 537(7619), S63. The genuine successes (imatinib, EGFR-targeted lung cancer therapy, and others) are not in dispute; the argument is over how representative those successes are of precision oncology's overall track record, and whether the field's rhetoric has run ahead of its results for the majority of patients.

categories.label: Applications
categories.subtitle: Where the science leaves the lab

entries.label: CAR-T Cell Therapy
entries.hint: Patients with relapsed, chemotherapy-resistant B-cell leukaemia or lymphoma can have their own T cells removed, genetically engineered to target their specific cancer, and reinfused — turning a normally fatal relapse into a treatable, sometimes curable condition. The therapy is expensive, resource-intensive to manufacture individually for each patient, and carries real toxicity risks (cytokine release syndrome), but represents a genuinely new treatment modality rather than a refinement of chemotherapy.
entries.note: Maude, S.L., Laetsch, T.W., Buechner, J., et al. (2018). 'Tisagenlecleucel in Children and Young Adults with B-Cell Lymphoblastic Leukemia.' New England Journal of Medicine, 378(5), 439–448 — the pivotal trial behind tisagenlecleucel's 2017 FDA approval (see st10 for the founding case report). Now approved for several blood cancer types; manufacturing cost and access remain significant real-world barriers.

entries.label: Checkpoint Inhibitor Immunotherapy
entries.hint: Drugs that block CTLA-4 or PD-1/PD-L1 release a brake the tumour was using to hide from the immune system, letting a patient's own T cells attack their cancer — durable, sometimes years-long remissions in a subset of patients with cancers that were previously almost uniformly fatal at an advanced stage. The catch is that most patients don't respond, and reliably predicting who will remains an unsolved clinical problem.
entries.note: Hodi, F.S., O'Day, S.J., McDermott, D.F., et al. (2010). 'Improved Survival with Ipilimumab in Patients with Metastatic Melanoma.' New England Journal of Medicine, 363(8), 711–723 — the pivotal trial behind ipilimumab's 2011 FDA approval (see fg10, fg11 for the underlying basic-science discoveries). Checkpoint inhibitors are now standard therapy across dozens of cancer types, though response rates vary enormously by tumour type.

entries.label: mRNA Vaccines
entries.hint: Instead of injecting a weakened or inactivated pathogen, an mRNA vaccine delivers genetic instructions that make the body's own cells briefly manufacture a harmless viral protein, training the immune system to recognise the real thing. The platform existed for decades before COVID-19 but was blocked by the immune system destroying unmodified synthetic RNA before it could work — solving that one chemistry problem unlocked the fastest vaccine development and deployment in history.
entries.note: Karikó, K., Weissman, D. (2005). 'Suppressing RNA Recognition by Toll-like Receptors: The Impact of Nucleoside Modification and the Evolutionary Origin of RNA.' Immunity, 23(2), 165–175 — the nucleoside-modification discovery that made mRNA vaccines clinically viable, for which Karikó and Weissman received the 2023 Nobel Prize. Deployed at unprecedented scale during the COVID-19 pandemic (Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna vaccines); the platform's speed and flexibility are now being applied to other infectious diseases and cancer vaccines.

entries.label: Monoclonal Antibody Therapeutics
entries.hint: A monoclonal antibody manufactured to bind one specific target can be turned into a drug in its own right — blocking a growth receptor on cancer cells, neutralising an inflammatory cytokine in autoimmune disease, or marking a pathogen for destruction. Trastuzumab, targeting the HER2 growth receptor overexpressed in a subset of aggressive breast cancers, was one of the first demonstrations that this approach could meaningfully extend survival, not just work in theory.
entries.note: Slamon, D.J., Leyland-Jones, B., Shak, S., et al. (2001). 'Use of Chemotherapy plus a Monoclonal Antibody against HER2 for Metastatic Breast Cancer That Overexpresses HER2.' New England Journal of Medicine, 344(11), 783–792. Built directly on the hybridoma technique (see me5); monoclonal antibody drugs now span oncology, autoimmune disease (e.g. adalimumab for rheumatoid arthritis), and infectious disease, and represent one of the largest drug categories by revenue in modern medicine.

entries.label: Population Cancer Screening Programmes
entries.hint: Systematically screening large, mostly healthy populations for early signs of cancer — mammography for breast cancer, colonoscopy for colorectal cancer, Pap smears for cervical cancer — catches some cancers early enough that treatment is far more likely to succeed. The Health Insurance Plan of New York trial was among the first to test this rigorously with a randomised design rather than simply assuming earlier detection must help.
entries.note: Shapiro, S., Strax, P., Venet, L. (1971). 'Periodic Breast Cancer Screening in Reducing Mortality from Breast Cancer.' JAMA, 215(11), 1777–1785 — one of the earliest randomised mammography screening trials. Screening programmes are now standard public health infrastructure in most high-income countries, though the overdiagnosis and lead-time-bias questions raised in db4 mean screening guidelines (which ages, how often) remain genuinely contested and periodically revised.

entries.label: Antibiotic Stewardship Programmes
entries.hint: Hospitals and health systems now run formal programmes to limit unnecessary or overly broad antibiotic use — the direct clinical countermeasure to the evolutionary reality that every prescription is also a selection event favouring resistant bacteria. The approach doesn't create new antibiotics; it tries to preserve the effectiveness of the ones that already exist for as long as possible.
entries.note: Dellit, T.H., Owens, R.C., McGowan, J.E., et al. (2007). 'Infectious Diseases Society of America and the Society for Healthcare Epidemiology of America Guidelines for Developing an Institutional Program to Enhance Antimicrobial Stewardship.' Clinical Infectious Diseases, 44(2), 159–177. Stewardship programmes have measurably slowed local resistance trends where implemented, but resistance continues to rise globally, and the pipeline of genuinely new antibiotic classes has been thin for decades — stewardship buys time rather than solving the underlying problem.

entries.label: Organ Transplant Immunosuppression
entries.hint: Preventing a transplant recipient's immune system from rejecting a donor organ as foreign tissue requires deliberately suppressing the same adaptive immune mechanisms that would otherwise protect the patient — a direct clinical application of tolerance theory. The introduction of cyclosporine transformed transplant survival rates by suppressing rejection more selectively than earlier blunt immunosuppressive drugs, without disabling the immune system as completely.
entries.note: Calne, R.Y., White, D.J.G., Thiru, S., et al. (1978). 'Cyclosporin A initially as the only immunosuppressant in 34 recipients of cadaveric organs: 32 kidneys, 2 pancreases, and 2 livers.' The Lancet, 312(8104), 1323–1327. One-year kidney graft survival rose from roughly 50% in the pre-cyclosporine era to over 80% afterward; recipients still require lifelong immunosuppression and face elevated infection and cancer risk as a direct trade-off of the therapy.

events.l: Establishment of the HeLa Cell Line
First Pediatric CAR-T Trial for Leukemia
Loss of contact inhibition in cancer
